text: This is a joint committee meeting of
public safety and economic development.
We're here to discuss the uh terms of
the new Axon contract for license plate
readers. I am joined by councelor Nav,
Councelor Moore, Councelor Williams,
President Piagua, Joe Barry, Sue
Katzoff, and Chief Trudeau. Um so who
wants to start? Chief, you want to
start?
>> Yeah, I'll start. First of all, thanks
for uh having us here. Also want to
thank the council for working with us on
this so we don't have a gap in uh LPR
service between our current existing
LPRs and going to Axon. It's very
important to us. So, thank you. Um so, I
thought we'd kick it off by going over
the uh the Axon
um terms.
>> Um I think I think what we want to do um
because the terms until we actually sign
a contract are not uh for public
dissemination. it would be considered
attorney client privilege. Let's just
talk about in general what the product
provides for SPD.
>> Okay. So, uh what we what we'd like is
uh you know these 26 uh LPR readers um
for public safety and for
investigations. Um basically what the
product does um depending on the uh the
install it goes on a light pole or it
goes on a fixed pole. Um it uh has a
camera that's uh reads multiple lanes of
traffic. It reads um cars going at uh
various speeds, highway speeds, traffic
speeds, and then compiles that data into
a system that allows the police
department to go back and based on
criminal investigations
search that data to assist us in, you
know, solving crime. So, that's the the
basic uh premise behind the LPRs and and
what SPD is looking for uh in their use.
And and I would like to note because I
think there are a few points that are um
vitally [clears throat] important for
this body and the public to understand.
Um the data that is collected as a
result of the installation of LPRs is
data that is solely owned and accessed
by SPD in the city. It is not owned or
controlled by the vendor. they provide a
clo what's called a closed system so
that only the data is only being uh
transmitted to SPD for their use. So I
think that that is an important feature
um that may be of import. So they can't
share data because they don't have data
to share the data only lives within SPD.
>> Thanks for sharing that Sue that that is
a huge concern in our community. So
thank you. Um, one [clears throat]
question I do have, Deputy Chief, is um,
CRI with our crime rates, um, how how
many crimes do you estimate that we're
solving because of these cameras?
>> You took my question.
>> Oh, I'm so sorry. [laughter] I stole his
question.
>> So, percentage-wise, I can't I don't
have a number percentage- wise. That'd
be an anecdotal number. But for our
major crimes, there's almost any no
homicides at all that don't happen to
have a vehicle involved in them. So I
can tell you that for our major crimes
they're used frequently. I think Axon
put a number at 70% of crimes overall
their number is a vehicle is involved in
the crime. Um so you know for our major
crimes the percentage is high and I
think overall that that number is is
pretty accurate you know based on that.
In thinking about evidence and closing
cases,
I imagine that in order to,
you know, go to a prosecutor, you have
to have a number of different points of
evidence.
How critical would LPR data be compared
to all of the other data that you would
bring to a prosecutor and what does that
look like?
>> Yeah. So, I mean, I think I think you
know there's going to be witness victim
statements. All right. And we all know
how how those identifications or witness
statements can go in terms of that. I
think this LPR data is factbased, right?
It takes a review by an officer, right?
I mean, we're not just going by the
read. We have to go into the system, but
we're actually looking at that plate.
We're looking at that car. We know that
car was there. And I think what it does
is it opens us up to additional leads.
That's what the LPR system does, right?
I think once we know that car, now we
have an avenue to get into potential
other people. Now we can track that car
to other locations where there may they
go into a store or there may be other
evidence or they say they purchase
something. We're we're looking at, you
know, those financial records. So I
think that's very important in
establishing that first lead that allows
detectives and officers to build from
there. But it's not just the lead,
right? There's a review process that
takes place based on that that um that
hit.
>> Another question I have, and again, this
is probably just to quell the public's
concerns.
>> Sure.
>> Um you're not staring at these cameras
looking for crime, right? These you're
going to the cameras once a crime's been
committed and you're trying to use them
to solve it, right?
>> Yeah, that's a great question. There's
no active monitoring of the LPR cameras
like our cops cameras. Um so this is uh
after the fact. um you know, somebody
provides a partial plate, somebody
provides a location where it occurred
and we're going in and we're
specifically looking at that time frame.
We're specifically looking at the
location where this might have occurred
and um there's no active monitoring.
>> Thank you.
>> So, I think the
the hot topic, right? What can we talk

comes from the LPRs? And this might be a
joint question. If we get a request from
a federal immigration agency to access
that data, what that looks like on our
end if they ask for it?
>> Yeah. So, I think um this came up in the
surveillance committee's review. Um and
absent a court order or a subpoena
in, you know, from uh ICE or a federal
entity, we will not share that that
information.
>> And that's Sorry, I'm stealing your
phone. Go ahead. That subpoena goes to
who, Sue? If they were to subpoena the
data looking for person. [clears throat]
>> So because the data is owned and housed
only with SPD,
I can't tell you that the vendor
wouldn't receive a subpoena, but they
don't have the information to supply
back. So they would let us know that
they received a subpoena, but only SPD
has data that would be responsive to
that subpoena. So it would have to come
through this office in order to be
responsive to that subpoena. Does that
answer your question?
>> And that's the only way we'll be would
be sharing.
>> Correct.
>> Have no other choice. I have
>> correct for the federal entities. That's
correct. Y
>> and chief now let's you know take the
federal government out of the equation.
What if we're talking about dit or
manest or New York state? What what does
that process look like? So right now
that our current footprint is that we're
not sharing information with uh those
other agencies. We would like to enter
into MOAS down the line that says we'll

allowed to share that data beyond just
yourself. I think there is value in the
collective law enforcement community
within Anadaga County cuz crime doesn't
stop at our borders um to share that
information locally for criminal
prosecutions.
But that agreement which we still have
to work out would be just for that
agency that we're working with. There's
no sharing of that information beyond
that entity to ICE or to other agencies.
And I think what you're saying, chief,
is the request would have to be
specific, tied to a crime at on a date
at an area. It's not, hey, we want to
see your LPR data for the month of
January. That's not what chief is
talking about. It would be tied to a
specific crime that had occurred and the
adjacent law enforcement agency locally
is trying to solve that incident
>> and with so along the same road. So with
in the past with previous
surveyors, we had for a year or so
accidentally or not accidentally left a
button on on that website that was
sharing our data with other agencies
across the country. Can we be assured
that with this new one, [snorts] none of
those buttons are on?
>> Yeah, that's absolutely the case. This
is an optin situation, not an opt out.
So as a standard, you're not sharing
with anyone unless you check the box to
do that. So we are assuring you that
we're not sharing any of that data with
the folks that or the entities that
you're concerned with. And I just want a
point of clarification.
It for local agencies our footprint
would be if we are able to share that
data so that they can come into their
systems and look at our data on an
ongoing basis if that's not something
that we want to do. But that would be
our position because I I can assure you
that with Dwit, the sheriff's
department, um you know, towns and
villages, again, crime is so transient
between our small community that if
able, we would like to share that data,
but to have safeguards in place in an
MOA that says you guys can't share it
with somebody else. That would be where
we'd like to go, but that is not where
we're at now. And there'll be a lot of
discussions to get there. I just wanted
to clarify that. no sharing with federal
agencies at all. But Anadaga County as a
community, I think, benefits from a
sharing to some degree of law
enforcement data that's got guard rails
and is protected.
>> I want to clarify our data for the for
the license plate readers is however
hosted on Axon systems. Correct.
>> It's in a cloud. Yes,
>> it's it's in a cloud. So they physically
contain that data in a processing center
someplace. If there is a data breach,
what are they liable for?
>> It's uh I don't know the answer to that.
I don't know that
>> that would be something we would want to

would form one of the terms of the
contract.
>> Okay. So, let's talk about the numbers
of LPR. So, we currently have 13 in the
city. Now, the plan is the original plan
was to have 26, but they we didn't get
to there. The plan is to go back and
have 26.
>> Yes, it is. Yeah, we're looking for 26.
Do we have those locations in stone?
>> We're looking at the same locations that
were in the original. Um
>> Okay. Can we get those locations?
>> Absolutely.
>> Um
anybody have I don't want to step over.
>> Just one more. Um there's a lot of
concern in the community um about the
city at all doing business with any
companies that also do business with
ICE. So um after having discussions with
you I understand that there's very
limited amount of companies out there
that can provide these services to us.
You guys are already using Axon for your
body warn cameras and such. So I just
want to um if you could just talk on on
the fact that there's not a lot of other
thing companies out there that we can go
to for these services that we need in
our city which is why we are doing this.
>> Yeah. I think a lot of the
state-of-the-art law enforcement
technologies that we would like to use
are going to be used by other federal
and agencies including ICE. There's a
reason for that, right? Because they're
probably some of the best across the
nation. And to limit our ability to use
some of those best systems only hurts
the Syracuse community, right? It's not
like it's going to affect them selling
that product to ICE or any other entity
that we have a concern with, but it will
severely limit what SPD is able to do
because those technologies, like I said,
are some of the best. And that's the
reasons why they're used across, you
know, at what point and where does that
end? I mean, Microsoft products are used
across a variety of different agencies.
I just drove here in a Chevy. you know,
those potentially are also going to be
used by federal agencies, right? I think
there's maybe other ways to for the
council and that's up to you, but to to
handle that, but to limit our ability
just because another entity uses it only
hurts the Syracuse community from using
and prohibits us from using some of the
best law enforcement tools that are out
there.
>> Thank you.
>> This may have already [clears throat]
been said and I apologize for arriving
late to the meeting. Could you tell me
what you use these for and what specific
crimes they have been used to solve, if
any?
>> The LPRs?
>> Yes.
>> Yeah. So, we did talk about that a
little bit, but um right off the bat, I
think almost every single homicide that
we have has a vehicle involved, and
we've searched uh for those. Um but it
it really runs the gamut. I think we u
said that potentially 70% across the
nation crimes have vehicles involved. So
there is a lot of opportunity for these
LPR cameras to be used to solve crime
and thereby preventing crime when you
take those criminals.
>> But spec specifically for SPD.
>> Yeah, we're there's there's no limit on
it's just crimes. Um there's no limit
like we're not just using them for
violent crimes. It is crimes across the
board where a vehicle is involved. These
could potentially be used for. Could you
also um repeat uh for the counselor the
fact that you're not watching live feeds
that you're only doing you're only using
it as a tool when a crime or other
incident has occurred.
>> Yeah, there's a differentiation between
our cops cameras that we've had for
[clears throat] years. There's, you
know, potential live monitoring on that
for surveillances and stuff like that.
The LPR cameras are different. We only
use the data from those LPR cameras in a
well reactive manner, right? Um so that
you know we have a crime um we have
specific information about suspects in a
vehicle. We go in and search um that
there's no live monitoring um in terms
of the camera. We will get hits if uh if
a car goes by that's stolen or if
there's a hit on the car because it has
is tied to an abduction. There are hits
for that, but there's no live monitoring
for just the sake of uh looking at the
camera.
>> What length of time do we keep the the
actual footage?
>> Yeah, great question. Right now, our
policy says 30 days. Um the surveillance
working group, I think, had from 3
minutes to up to a year with the median
vote being 30 days. So, I think that's
that's, you know, an appropriate. We're
we're comfortable with that 30 days.
Chief,
>> is there a situation where you extend
that? So, let me just Right. If if you
have a homicide on a certain corner
where you know there's license plate
reader, do you request to keep data for
that corner for let's say you know let's
keep that longer because we may come up
on something.
>> Sure. That's exactly. So any anytime we
pull data from the system for a crime,
right? We're going to archive it off and
it becomes part of evidence. The
schedule for the retention of that
evidence follows the crime um in the in
the in the discovery for that crime. And
that that's all very much dependent on
the crime. murders, rapes, you have to
keep forever, right? And then as the
crime decreases to misdemeanor and so
that length of time that we need to keep
that shortens, but just the bulk data
that's not being used um in a criminal
prosecution or for evidence is 30 days
and then after that it follows the
retention based on the crime.
So, I wanted to just share the real life
example that you shared with me, the
woman [clears throat] on the north side
that was um killed I think a couple few
years ago, a couple years ago. Um that
was solved with cameras, right?
>> Yeah. Yeah. Yes.
>> So, I just want to make the public aware
that
>> Oh, yeah. I mean, they they are critical
in some of our larger crimes and in
piecing things together. I think I said
that, you know, almost every single
homicide we're using the LPR cameras.
So, so just to be clear, so once it hits
the archive
>> where you keep whatever length after

into whatever action
then those are erased at the
>> Yeah. So, let me let me clarify that.
Yeah, I'll expand on that. So, let's
